content.content:
2 - what is money?

2.1 - Money as a Medium of Exchange

Money is simply a medium used to exchange goods and services instead of conflict or barter.

It has no intrinsic value by itself.

It gains value only because the government certifies it as legal tender.

Without that certification, money is just paper.

2.2 - Core Institutions You Must Understand Before Going Deeper

2.2.1 - Central Bank

Every country has one central authority controlling money.

India has Reserve Bank of India (RBI)

USA has Federal Reserve

Think of it as the parent bank.

All other banks (SBI, ICICI, HDFC, etc.) operate under it.

2.2.2 - Government Account at Central Bank

Just like individuals have bank accounts, the government also has an account.

This account exists at the central bank.

2.2.3 - Credit or Crediting

To credit an account means adding money to it.

Example:

Your friend sends you ₹1,000.

Balance goes from ₹500 to ₹1,500.

The increase is called credit.

When a central bank credits money,

It is simply adding numbers digitally.

No physical printing happens at that moment.

2.3 - How the Economy Works, The High-Level Reality

It is critical to understand how money is created, circulated, and diluted.

This explains inflation, debt, liquidity, and why banks can fail.

2.3.1 - How Is Money Created?

There are two types of money creation.

2.3.1.1 - Physical Notes

Central bank prints notes.

Notes enter banks.

People withdraw cash.

This is a very small portion of total money.

2.3.1.2 - Digital Money Creation (Most Important)

Most money is not printed.

It is created digitally.

Process:

• Government needs money

• Taxes are insufficient

• Government asks central bank

• Central bank credits government account

• New money is created digitally

2.3.2 - Why Is Money Created?

Because governments spend more than they earn.

For infrastructure, salaries, subsidies, and crisis support.

Raising taxes has limits.

So money is created instead.

2.3.3 - How Do Banks Circulate Money?

Banks act as pipelines.

Process:

• People deposit money

• Banks keep a small reserve (example 4%)

• Banks lend the rest

Example:

Deposit ₹1,00,000

Bank keeps ₹4,000

Bank lends ₹96,000

That money is redeposited and lent again

This multiplies money in circulation.

2.3.4 - Why Does Inflation Exist?

Inflation means money losing value.

It exists because money supply grows faster than goods.

Example:

100 people, ₹10 each, one sandwich.

Price rises because money is weaker.

2.3.5 - Impact on the Economy

• Prices rise yearly

• Salaries lag behind

• Cash holders lose

• Asset holders gain

Balanced inflation keeps the system moving.

2.3.6 - Impact on Individuals

Keeping money in cash or low-interest savings leads to loss.

Example:

₹1,00,000 today buys 4 months of groceries.

Next year it buys only 3.5 months.

2.3.7 - What Can You Do?

The goal is to beat inflation.

Actions:

• Keep emergency cash only

• Invest long-term money into inflation-beating assets

• Build skills to increase income

Your goal is protection and growth, not just saving.
